name: Systems Administrator
summary: An IT specialist who installs, configures, and maintains servers, user accounts, backups, and operating systems for organizations — the backbone role of corporate computing since minicomputer departments.
duties: Patch servers, monitor uptime, manage AD or LDAP directories, restore backups, automate routine jobs, and support infrastructure tickets.
skills: Linux or Windows server administration, scripting, networking basics, incident response, documentation.
tools: SSH, Ansible, monitoring stacks, virtualization, ticket queues.
regions: Global enterprises, schools, hospitals, and government agencies.
educationPath: Computer science or IT certifications (e.g. Linux+, cloud vendor certs).
modernSectors: Enterprise IT, managed service providers, SaaS infrastructure teams.

events.label: Linux in the data center
events.description: Open-source server stacks expand sysadmin specialization beyond mainframe operations.

sources.title: Corporate computing histories
sources.note: Minicomputer era operations groups precede modern sysadmin titles.

name: Network Engineer
summary: A technician who designs and maintains LANs, WANs, routing, switching, and firewall policies — professionalized as TCP/IP networks became the default corporate backbone.
duties: Configure routers and switches, troubleshoot outages, plan capacity, document topology, and implement security zones.
skills: Routing protocols, subnetting, firewall rules, vendor CLI fluency, physical cabling literacy.
tools: Cisco/Juniper gear, Wireshark, network monitoring, SD-WAN controllers.
regions: Global; every organization with more than a handful of computers.
educationPath: Network certifications (CCNA and equivalents) plus on-the-job training.
modernSectors: ISPs, enterprises, cloud providers, data centers.

events.label: Internet in business
events.description: WAN connectivity and firewalls become standard enterprise network engineering work.

name: Database Administrator
summary: A specialist who installs, tunes, backs up, and secures relational and NoSQL databases — critical once organizations stored payroll, inventory, and customer records in shared DBMS platforms.
duties: Manage schemas, tune queries, schedule backups, patch DBMS software, plan replication, and recover from corruption events.
skills: SQL, indexing, capacity planning, security hardening, disaster recovery drills.
tools: PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL, cloud managed databases.
regions: Global enterprises and SaaS vendors.
educationPath: Computer science or DBA certification tracks.
modernSectors: Finance, retail, government, cloud database services.

events.label: Managed cloud databases
events.description: RDS-style services shift some patching and scaling tasks toward vendors.

name: DevOps Engineer
summary: A role bridging development and operations that automates build, test, and deployment pipelines — named explicitly in the 2000s as web services demanded frequent releases.
duties: Maintain CI/CD, infrastructure as code, observability, incident runbooks, and collaboration between dev and ops teams.
skills: Scripting, containers, cloud APIs, monitoring, release coordination, security basics.
tools: Kubernetes, Terraform, GitHub Actions, Jenkins, Prometheus.
regions: Tech companies and digital teams in traditional enterprises worldwide.
educationPath: Software engineering or sysadmin background with cloud tooling.
modernSectors: SaaS, fintech, e-commerce, platform teams.

events.label: Container orchestration
events.description: Docker and Kubernetes standardize how DevOps teams package and ship services.

sources.title: DevOps practice literature
sources.note: Term popularized after Patrick Debois DevOpsDays 2009.

name: Cloud Engineer
summary: An engineer who architects and operates workloads on public cloud platforms — a distinct specialty after AWS, Azure, and GCP became default enterprise infrastructure choices.
duties: Design VPCs, migrate legacy apps, manage IAM, optimize cloud spend, and enforce security baselines.
skills: Cloud vendor services, networking, automation, cost governance, compliance mapping.
tools: AWS/Azure/GCP consoles, Terraform, CloudFormation, cost dashboards.
regions: Global; concentrated where enterprises adopt cloud-first policies.
originLabel: Amazon Web Services commercial launch era
educationPath: Vendor cloud certifications plus software or sysadmin experience.
modernSectors: Enterprise migration programs, startups, government cloud adoption.

events.label: Enterprise cloud migration
events.description: Large firms begin moving datacenter workloads to hyperscale providers.

name: Site Reliability Engineer
summary: An engineer who applies software practices to operations — error budgets, SLOs, and on-call rotation — codified at Google and adopted by large web platforms.
duties: Define reliability targets, automate toil, run incident response, postmortems, and capacity planning for high-traffic services.
skills: Programming, statistics, distributed systems debugging, communication under outage pressure.
tools: Monitoring, paging systems, feature flags, chaos testing frameworks.
regions: Large internet companies and enterprises imitating SRE practices.
educationPath: Software engineering background; SRE handbooks and on-call training.
modernSectors: Hyperscale web, fintech, streaming, cloud-native SaaS.

events.label: SRE book popularized
events.description: Google SRE practices spread as a named job family outside original company.

sources.title: Site Reliability Engineering (O'Reilly)
sources.note: Google-origin discipline for operating large services.

name: Cybersecurity Analyst
summary: A defender who monitors threats, investigates alerts, and hardens systems — professionalized as networks connected to the public internet and compliance regimes matured.
duties: Triage SIEM alerts, patch vulnerabilities, run phishing simulations, document incidents, and support forensic handoffs.
skills: Threat modeling, log analysis, networking, policy writing, calm under incident stress.
tools: SIEM, EDR, vulnerability scanners, firewall logs, ticketing.
regions: Global; mandatory in regulated finance, health, and government IT.
educationPath: Security certifications (Security+, CISSP paths) and IT experience.
modernSectors: SOC teams, MSSPs, corporate security, critical infrastructure.

events.label: SOC formalization
events.description: Security operations centers become standard in large enterprises.

name: Data Scientist
summary: A quantitative analyst who builds models and experiments on large datasets — named as a distinct tech role in the 2010s big-data and machine-learning hiring wave.
duties: Frame business questions, clean data, train models, evaluate metrics, and communicate findings to product and leadership teams.
skills: Statistics, Python or R, experiment design, visualization, domain curiosity.
tools: Jupyter, SQL, scikit-learn, cloud ML platforms, experiment trackers.
regions: Tech hubs, finance, retail analytics, healthcare research.
educationPath: Statistics, computer science, or quantitative PhDs; bootcamps for career changers.
modernSectors: Tech, advertising, insurance, logistics optimization.

events.label: Big data hiring boom
events.description: LinkedIn and media popularize data scientist as a hot tech title.

sources.title: Harvard Business Review — data scientist
sources.note: Widely cited 2012 article on the role's rise.

name: Machine Learning Engineer
summary: An engineer who productionizes models — training pipelines, serving infrastructure, and monitoring drift — distinct from research scientists who focus on novel algorithms.
duties: Build feature pipelines, deploy model endpoints, optimize inference cost, and maintain ML observability in production.
skills: Software engineering, ML frameworks, data engineering, performance tuning.
tools: PyTorch, TensorFlow, feature stores, Kubernetes, MLflow.
regions: Tech companies with recommendation, search, or vision products.
educationPath: Computer science or ML specialization; often from data science or backend roles.
modernSectors: Search, ads, autonomous systems, enterprise AI features.

events.label: Generative model products
events.description: LLM APIs push more teams to ship AI features requiring MLOps discipline.

name: Data Analyst
summary: A worker who queries databases, builds dashboards, and answers business questions with numbers — expanded dramatically as SQL and BI tools spread beyond statistics departments.
duties: Write SQL, maintain dashboards, define metrics, present trends, and validate data quality with stakeholders.
skills: SQL, spreadsheet modeling, visualization, business communication, curiosity.
tools: SQL clients, Tableau, Looker, Excel, Python notebooks.
regions: Every industry with digital operations.
educationPath: Business, economics, or analytics programs; SQL bootcamps.
modernSectors: Product analytics, marketing ops, finance, operations.

events.label: Self-serve BI
events.description: Dashboard tools let non-engineers query curated datasets directly.

name: Software QA Engineer
summary: A quality specialist who designs test plans and automates regression checks for software releases — institutionalized as applications grew too large for developer-only testing.
duties: Write test cases, build automation frameworks, file defects, verify fixes, and sign off release candidates.
skills: Test design, scripting, attention to detail, risk prioritization, API testing.
tools: Selenium, Cypress, JUnit, test management systems, CI hooks.
regions: Software companies worldwide; often colocated with dev teams.
educationPath: Computer science or QA certification; manual testing entry paths.
modernSectors: Enterprise software, SaaS, embedded systems, games.

events.label: Test automation growth
events.description: Agile teams embed QA automation alongside continuous integration.

name: IT Support Specialist
summary: A frontline technician who resets passwords, troubleshoots laptops, and escalates outages — the help-desk layer every organization with PCs has maintained since the 1980s.
duties: Answer tickets, image machines, install software, guide users, and route complex issues to sysadmins or vendors.
skills: Troubleshooting, patience, ticketing hygiene, basic networking and OS knowledge.
tools: Remote desktop, MDM consoles, knowledge bases, asset trackers.
regions: Global offices, schools, hospitals, call centers.
educationPath: CompTIA A+ and help-desk experience; internal promotion to sysadmin.
modernSectors: Corporate IT, MSPs, university computing, retail tech support.

events.label: Remote work surge
events.description: VPN and laptop logistics expand distributed help-desk workloads.

name: UX Designer
summary: A designer who researches user needs and shapes interfaces for software products — named distinctly as graphical user interfaces and web apps required usability specialization.
duties: Interview users, wireframe flows, run usability tests, hand off specs to engineering, and iterate from analytics.
skills: User research, information architecture, prototyping, accessibility awareness, collaboration.
tools: Figma, Sketch, usability labs, analytics dashboards.
regions: Tech product companies globally; agency studios.
educationPath: HCI programs, design bootcamps, portfolio-driven hiring.
modernSectors: SaaS, mobile apps, e-commerce, government digital services.

events.label: Mobile UX
events.description: Smartphones create dedicated mobile interaction design workflows.

name: Technical Writer
summary: A communicator who documents APIs, admin guides, and release notes for technical audiences — essential once software products shipped to customers who never met the engineers.
duties: Draft manuals, maintain docs sites, interview SMEs, version documentation with releases, and enforce style guides.
skills: Clear writing, diagramming, tooling literacy, audience modeling, version control for docs.
tools: Markdown docs stacks, MadCap Flare, Confluence, OpenAPI specs.
regions: Software vendors, open-source projects, hardware manufacturers.
educationPath: English or technical communication degrees; engineer-to-writer career paths.
modernSectors: Developer tools, enterprise SaaS, regulated hardware.

events.label: Docs-as-code
events.description: Engineers and writers publish docs from repositories beside source code.

name: Solutions Architect
summary: A senior technologist who designs how products fit a customer's stack — common at enterprise vendors selling complex platforms that require pre-sales engineering.
duties: Map requirements, propose architectures, estimate effort, support sales demos, and guide implementation teams.
skills: Broad stack knowledge, communication, trade-off reasoning, security and compliance awareness.
tools: Architecture diagrams, cloud reference designs, POC environments.
regions: Enterprise software and cloud vendor customer teams globally.
educationPath: Senior engineering or consulting background.
modernSectors: Cloud providers, ERP vendors, systems integrators.

events.label: Cloud solution selling
events.description: Vendors formalize architect roles for migration and landing-zone designs.

name: Frontend Developer
summary: A developer who implements user interfaces in browsers or native UI layers — specialized as web apps grew richer than static HTML pages.
duties: Build components, manage state, optimize rendering, ensure accessibility, and integrate with backend APIs.
skills: JavaScript/TypeScript, CSS, component frameworks, performance profiling, design collaboration.
tools: React, Vue, bundlers, browser devtools, Storybook.
regions: Global product teams and agencies.
educationPath: Bootcamps, computer science, or self-taught web portfolios.
modernSectors: SaaS, e-commerce, media, design-heavy startups.

events.label: Component frameworks
events.description: React and similar libraries make frontend engineering a distinct specialty.

name: Backend Developer
summary: A developer who builds servers, APIs, databases, and background jobs — the counterpart to frontend work in split web engineering teams.
duties: Design APIs, implement business logic, tune databases, handle authentication, and operate services in production.
skills: Server languages, SQL, caching, distributed systems basics, security hygiene.
tools: Node, Java, Go, Postgres, Redis, message queues.
regions: Global software organizations.
educationPath: Computer science or backend-focused bootcamps.
modernSectors: SaaS APIs, fintech, marketplaces, infrastructure products.

events.label: REST API economy
events.description: Mobile apps and SaaS integrations expand dedicated API engineering.

name: Mobile App Developer
summary: A developer who ships applications for smartphones and tablets — a distinct job family after iOS and Android app stores created commercial mobile software markets.
duties: Implement screens, integrate device APIs, optimize battery and network use, publish to app stores, and fix crash reports.
skills: Swift/Kotlin or cross-platform frameworks, mobile UX patterns, release signing, performance tuning.
tools: Xcode, Android Studio, Flutter, Firebase, app analytics.
regions: Global; product companies and contract studios.
educationPath: Mobile development courses; often from web or backend backgrounds.
modernSectors: Consumer apps, banking, delivery, health tech.

events.label: App Store opens
events.description: Commercial mobile app development becomes a mass occupation.

name: Embedded Software Engineer
summary: An engineer who writes firmware for microcontrollers and devices with tight resource limits — distinct from application developers working on servers or browsers.
duties: Write C/C++ firmware, debug hardware interfaces, meet real-time constraints, and certify safety-critical behavior.
skills: Low-level programming, electronics literacy, RTOS concepts, testing on hardware.
tools: JTAG debuggers, oscilloscopes, cross-compilers, CI for firmware.
regions: Automotive, aerospace, medical device, consumer electronics hubs.
educationPath: Electrical engineering or computer engineering degrees.
modernSectors: Automotive, IoT, industrial control, medical implants.

events.label: IoT expansion
events.description: Connected devices broaden embedded software hiring beyond classic industrial firms.

name: Systems Analyst
summary: An IT professional who translated business requirements into specifications for mainframe and enterprise systems — peak role in 1970s–1990s corporate computing before agile product teams absorbed much of the function.
duties: Gather requirements, document flows, coordinate vendors, and supervise programmers on large batch projects.
skills: Process mapping, structured analysis methods, stakeholder interviews, cost estimation.
tools: DFD diagrams, CASE tools, Gantt charts, COBOL-era documentation.
regions: US and European corporate IT departments historically.
educationPath: MIS degrees and internal promotion from programming pools.
modernSectors: Legacy enterprise modernization, government IT, consulting.

events.label: Agile product teams
events.description: Iterative software teams reduce standalone analyst roles on many projects.
